common_name: Black Magic Taro (Dramatic Near-Black Elephant Ear for Tropical Gardens and Bogs)

planting.note: Plant tubers or container plants after the last frost in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Black Magic develops the most dramatically dark, near-black leaf color with some direct sun — deep shade produces larger but less intensely dark leaves. Tolerates standing water and is excellent at water garden margins.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Black Magic is a stunning Colocasia esculenta cultivar producing dramatically large, heart-shaped, velvety leaves of deep purple-black to near-black — the darkest, most intensely colored of the commonly grown elephant ear cultivars, creating a bold tropical accent that provides extraordinary contrast against light-colored or green-foliaged companions. The leaves emerge with a glossy, almost iridescent deep purple-black sheen that makes them appear more like a dark fabric than conventional plant foliage; the color is intense enough that the leaves absorb rather than reflect light, creating a visual weight in the garden that lighter plants cannot achieve. Black Magic grows as a medium-to-large clumping tropical perennial to 3–5 feet, producing new leaves continuously through the growing season. Like all Colocasia esculenta varieties, Black Magic is adapted to moist and wet soils — it can grow in standing water up to 6 inches deep, making it outstanding for water garden margins, bog gardens, pond edges, and any consistently wet low area in the garden. In Gulf South zone 9, Black Magic grows as a perennial if given winter mulch protection — the tubers survive most zone 9 winters with mulching, returning vigorously each spring. The leaves are mildly toxic if eaten without cooking due to calcium oxalate crystals; the tubers of the Colocasia species are edible when cooked but Black Magic is grown primarily as an ornamental rather than for food. Outstanding combined with chartreuse, gold, or white companion plants.

common_name: Little Henry Virginia Sweetspire (Compact Fragrant White Native Shrub for Partial Shade)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, well-drained to moderately wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Little Henry spreads slowly by root suckers to form an expanding colony — a desirable quality for naturalizing in shrub borders, but plant where the slow spread can be accommodated.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Little Henry is a compact selection of Virginia Sweetspire (Itea virginica) — one of the most valuable and reliable native shrubs for eastern North American shade and partial-shade gardens, providing three distinct seasons of ornamental interest in a small, manageable size suitable for mixed borders and foundation plantings. In late spring to early summer (May–June in zone 9), Little Henry produces long, arching, fragrant white flower racemes (3–4 inches) that droop gracefully from the branches, providing light sweetly-scented bloom when many shrubs have finished flowering. The summer foliage is clean, medium-green, and disease-free. Then in autumn, Little Henry delivers one of the most reliable and vivid fall color displays of any native shrub adapted to Gulf South conditions — the leaves turn scarlet, burgundy, purple-red, and orange, often holding the fall color longer than most other shrubs and providing persistent autumn interest through October and into November in zone 9. The compact habit (2–3 feet versus 4–5 feet for the standard species) makes Little Henry more versatile in typical garden settings. Virginia Sweetspire is native to moist woods and stream banks throughout the eastern US, including Gulf Coast regions, providing excellent ecological value — it is a larval host for several moths and provides wildlife habitat through the winter.

common_name: Palace Purple Coral Bells (AAS Deep Bronze-Purple Foliage Shade Perennial)

planting.note: Plant container plants or divisions in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Palace Purple develops its best deep bronze-purple foliage color in partial shade with some morning sun — full deep shade dulls the color toward brownish-green, while intense afternoon sun in zone 9 can scorch the leaves.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Palace Purple is the original All-America Selections Heuchera winner that launched the contemporary interest in foliage-first coral bells breeding — producing distinctive broad, maple-like leaves in deep bronze-purple to burgundy-red with metallic overtones that hold their color through the growing season in appropriate conditions, brightening shaded garden beds with warm, rich color unavailable from most other shade perennials. The foliage is the primary ornamental feature: the deeply lobed, mahogany-bronze leaves are striking in partial-shade borders where they contrast with green or chartreuse companions. Tall, airy scapes of small cream-white to pale pink flowers appear above the foliage in late spring, providing delicate vertical interest and nectar for small pollinators. Palace Purple is grown primarily from seed and shows some variability in foliage color intensity — the best seedlings display deep, uniform bronze-purple while weaker selections tend toward greenish-bronze. In Gulf South conditions, Heuchera is somewhat heat-sensitive — zone 9 is at the warm edge of reliable performance, and siting in morning-sun-afternoon-shade positions with consistent moisture is essential. Well-drained soil that avoids waterlogging is critical: crown rot in poorly drained or waterlogged soil is the primary cause of heuchera failure. Division every 2–3 years when the crown becomes woody and elevated from the soil surface rejuvenates the plant and maintains vigor.

common_name: Moon Vine (Large-Flowered White Night-Blooming Tropical Vine for Evening Gardens)

planting.note: Nick or soak seeds overnight before planting to speed germination; direct sow after the last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Moon Vine is best planted where it can climb a trellis, fence, or arbor and be viewed in the evening when the flowers open — the flowers close by mid-morning each day.

directions: Nick or soak seeds overnight, then direct sow after the last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Moon Vine (Ipomoea alba) is the classic evening and night garden vine — a tropical twining vine that produces extraordinarily large (4–6 inch), pure white, funnel-shaped flowers that open in late afternoon and evening, perfuming the garden with a sweet, rich, jasmine-like fragrance that intensifies as darkness falls. The flowers are among the largest of any commonly grown annual vine and the pure white color reflects low light beautifully, appearing to glow under moonlight, porch lights, or landscape lighting — creating the ethereal moonlit effect that gives the plant its common name. Moon Vine is a rapid, enthusiastic climber, gaining 15–20 feet in a single growing season in warm Gulf South conditions — it covers fences, arbors, pergolas, and mailboxes with dense, large-leaved foliage and a continuous supply of evening flowers from mid-summer until frost. The flowers each last only one evening, but numerous buds open in succession for months of nightly bloom. In Gulf South zone 9, Moon Vine is grown as a warm-season annual from spring through fall. In zone 10–12, it grows as a perennial twining vine. The rapid growth and profuse bloom make it one of the most rewarding evening-garden plants for summer arbors and screens. Often combined with common morning glory (I. purpurea) to create a 24-hour floral display from the same genus.

common_name: Rose Queen Spider Flower (Classic Rose-Pink Self-Seeding Annual for Summer Borders)

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost (or in early fall in zone 9 for winter-spring bloom) in well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun; Rose Queen self-sows prolifically — once established, it will return as volunteers each year without replanting in most Gulf South gardens, though seedlings are variable in color.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost in well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun; Rose Queen is a rose-pink selection of Cleome hassleriana (spider flower) — one of the most heat-tolerant, drought-resistant, self-sufficient large annuals for Gulf South summer gardens, producing large, airy flower heads of rose-pink to warm-pink flowers with long, protruding stamens that give each floret its characteristic spider-like appearance. Cleome is uniquely suited to Gulf South summer heat: it grows vigorously through July and August when other summer annuals stall or decline, reaching 4–5 feet and producing continuous bloom from the elongating flower head at the top of each stem. The slightly sticky, aromatic stems and foliage are characteristic of the species and can be sticky to the touch — a minor trait that does not detract from the plant's considerable garden value. The large flower heads, combined with the tall habit and self-sowing tendency, create a semi-naturalized effect in borders that successive generations of gardeners have found to be exactly what hot, difficult summer borders need — a tall, self-sustaining plant that provides color and structure without requiring constant attention. Rose Queen produces predominantly rose-pink flowers but may show some color variation in self-sown seedlings, which can include white ('Alba') and lavender-purple forms within the same planting. An outstanding plant for butterflies and sphinx moths, which are particularly effective at accessing the long-stamened flowers. In Gulf South zone 9, Cleome can be fall-sown for spring bloom.
